title: W4 A Sequences

1/19  Sequences and Limits

(b) {xk }∞ k k k=1 such that x = 0 if k is odd and x = 1 if k is even.

(c) {y k }∞ k k=1 such that y = k.

(d) {z k }∞ k 1 k=1 such that z = k .

Convergent Sequences

Definition 2

k=1 converges to x ∈ R if for

every ϵ > 0 there exists some positive integer K such that k ≥ K =⇒ |xk − x| < ϵ.

If {xk }∞ k=1 converges to x, then x is called its limit.

4/19   Convergent Sequences

Definition 2

k=1 converges to x ∈ R if for

every ϵ > 0 there exists some positive integer K such that k ≥ K =⇒ |xk − x| < ϵ.

If {xk }∞ k=1 converges to x, then x is called its limit.

Continuing with Example 1: (a) {wk }∞ k k=1 converges to c; write w → c.

(b) {xk }∞ k=1 alternates between 0 and 1 (it does not have a limit).

(c) {y k }∞ k k=1 diverges, write y → ∞.

4/19   Example 1 (d)

Claim. The sequence {z k }∞ k=1 converges to 0:

lim z k = 0. k→∞

|z k − z| = 1 k − 0

5/19   Example 1 (d)

Claim. The sequence {z k }∞ k=1 converges to 0:

lim z k = 0. k→∞

◦ Fix ϵ > 0.

◦ Let K ∈ N be such that K > ? .

◦ For all k ≥ K we obtain

|z k − z| = 1 k − 0 = 1 k ≤ 1 K < ϵ.

◦ So, for ϵ > 0, choosing K > 1/ϵ shows that z k → z.

◦ Take for example K = 2/ϵ.

|z k − z| = 1 k ≤ 1 K = ϵ < ϵ.

9/19   A Useful Observation

Intuitively, if the limit of a sequence {xk }∞ k=1 is x, then the distance

between xk and x becomes negligible as k becomes large.

Proposition 2

k=1 converges to x ∈ R if, and only

if, lim xk − x = 0. k→∞

10/19   A Useful Observation

Intuitively, if the limit of a sequence {xk }∞ k=1 is x, then the distance

between xk and x becomes negligible as k becomes large.

Proposition 2

k=1 converges to x ∈ R if, and only

if, lim xk − x = 0. k→∞

Proof The equivalence follows directly from definitions. ◦ The sequence {|xk − x|}∞ k=1 converges to 0 if for any ϵ > 0 there is a

positive integer K such that for all k ≥ K, one has

|xk − x| − 0 = xk − x < ϵ.

◦ This is equivalent to saying that xk → x.

10/19  Properties of Limits of Sequences

11/19

Consider two sequences {xk }∞ k ∞ k=0 and {y }k=0 in R, with limits x and y:

xk → x and y k → y. ◦ Construct the sequence {z k }∞ k k k k=1 by setting z = x + y , for each k ∈ N.

◦ What can we say about the limit of {z k }∞ k=1 ?

12/19

Consider two sequences {xk }∞ k ∞ k=0 and {y }k=0 in R, with limits x and y:

xk → x and y k → y. ◦ Construct the sequence {z k }∞ k k k k=1 by setting z = x + y , for each k ∈ N.

◦ What can we say about the limit of {z k }∞ k=1 ?

Proposition 3

(a) xk + y k → x + y.

(b) α xk → α x, for all α ∈ R.

(c) xk y k → x y. k (d) If in addition y k ̸= 0 for all k ∈ N and y ̸= 0, then xyk → x y .

12/19

Proof of part (a) ◦ Fix ϵ > 0 and let K be such that for al k ≥ K, we have

|xk − x| < ϵ and |y k − y| < ϵ .

13/19

Proof of part (a) ◦ Fix ϵ > 0 and let K be such that for al k ≥ K, we have

|xk − x| < ϵ and |y k − y| < ϵ .

◦ Then for all k ≥ K, one has

|(xk + y k ) − (x + y)| ≤ |xk − x| + |y k − y|

13/19

Proof of part (a) ◦ Fix ϵ > 0 and let K be such that for al k ≥ K, we have

|xk − x| < ϵ and |y k − y| < ϵ .

◦ Then for all k ≥ K, one has

|(xk + y k ) − (x + y)| ≤ |xk − x| + |y k − y| ϵ < 2 + 2ϵ = ϵ.

◦ Thus xk + y k → x + y, as desired.

13/19

Proof of part (b) If α = 0, the sequence {α xk }∞ k=1 has constant value of zero.

Assume now that α ̸= 0. ◦ Fix ϵ > 0 and let K be a positive integer so that, for k ≥ K, one has

|xk − x| < ϵ |α| .

◦ For all such k, we can write

α xk − α x = |α| |xk − x| < ϵ.

Part (c) is left as exercise. Part (d) is a bit more difficult so we’ll take it for granted.

14/19   Limits Extend Well

Consider the sequence {xkj }∞ k=1 in R, with limit xj , for each j = 1, . . . , n.

15/19   Limits Extend Well

Consider the sequence {xkj }∞ k=1 in R, with limit xj , for each j = 1, . . . , n.

Write xk = (xk1 , xk2 , . . . , xkn ) ∈ Rn , and say that xk → x if and only if xkj → xj for each j = 1, . . . , n.

15/19   Limits Extend Well

Consider the sequence {xkj }∞ k=1 in R, with limit xj , for each j = 1, . . . , n.

Write xk = (xk1 , xk2 , . . . , xkn ) ∈ Rn , and say that xk → x if and only if xkj → xj for each j = 1, . . . , n.

Proposition 4

(a) xk + y k → x + y. (b) α xk → α x, for all α ∈ R. (c) xk · y k → x · y.

The proof of this proposition follows from previous results. Note that part (c) deals with a sequence of real numbers.

15/19  Closed Sets and Sequences

16/19   Another Look at Closed Sets

Sometimes it is convenient to use sequences when examining the

Proposition 5

The set V ⊆ Rn is closed if and only if for any sequence {xk }∞ k=1 such

that xk ∈ V for all k = 1, 2, . . ., if xk → x then x ∈ V .

17/19   Another Look at Closed Sets

Sometimes it is convenient to use sequences when examining the

Proposition 5

The set V ⊆ Rn is closed if and only if for any sequence {xk }∞ k=1 such

that xk ∈ V for all k = 1, 2, . . ., if xk → x then x ∈ V .

Example 3

V = [0, 1] and {xk = 1/k : k = 1, 2, . . .}. The limit of the sequence {xk } is 0, which belongs to V .

17/19

◦ Since x ∈ V c , and V c is open, there exists ϵ > 0 such that Bϵ (x) ⊆ V c .

◦ For such ϵ, there is K such that k ≥ K implies ∥xk − x∥ < ϵ.

◦ Thus xk ∈ V c for all k ≥ K, a contradiction.

◦ Since x ∈ V c , and V c is open, there exists ϵ > 0 such that Bϵ (x) ⊆ V c .

◦ For such ϵ, there is K such that k ≥ K implies ∥xk − x∥ < ϵ.

◦ Thus xk ∈ V c for all k ≥ K, a contradiction.

⇐= Suppose that for every sequence {xk } such that xk ∈ V for all k, xk → x implies x ∈ V . By contradiction, suppose that V is not closed.

◦ Since x ∈ V c , and V c is open, there exists ϵ > 0 such that Bϵ (x) ⊆ V c .

◦ For such ϵ, there is K such that k ≥ K implies ∥xk − x∥ < ϵ.

◦ Thus xk ∈ V c for all k ≥ K, a contradiction.

⇐= Suppose that for every sequence {xk } such that xk ∈ V for all k, xk → x implies x ∈ V . By contradiction, suppose that V is not closed. ◦ Then V c is not open.

◦ There exists some x̂ ∈ V c such that for each k = 1, 2, . . . one can find x̂k for which ∥x̂k − x̂∥ < k1 and x̂k ∈ / V c.

◦ Then x̂k ∈ V for each k and x̂k → x̂, yet x̂ ∈

Another Look at Bounded Sets

Sometimes it is convenient to use subsequences when examining

Proposition 6

The set V ⊆ Rn is bounded if and only if for any sequence {xk }∞ k=1

such that xk ∈ V for all k = 1, 2, . . . , there exists a subsequence {xkj }∞ n j=1 and some x ∈ R such that x kj → x.

19/19   Another Look at Bounded Sets

Sometimes it is convenient to use subsequences when examining

Proposition 6

The set V ⊆ Rn is bounded if and only if for any sequence {xk }∞ k=1

such that xk ∈ V for all k = 1, 2, . . . , there exists a subsequence {xkj }∞ n j=1 and some x ∈ R such that x kj → x.

Example 4

Let V = [−1, 1] and consider the sequence {xk } given by xk = (−1)k . This sequence does not converge, but it has convergent subsequences:

x2j = 1 → 1 and x2j−1 = −1 → −1.
